forecasts.reasoning: The borders in Kwara state (specifically Taberu to Tanu) were closed by Benin on May 7, 2025, due to alleged agricultural protectionism, arms smuggling, and the emergence of a new terror group (‘Mahmuda’). Rather than resolving the issue, Benin escalated the situation on May 21, 2025, by expanding the border clampdown to a waterway in Ogun State and key borders in Lagos (Sèmè, Owode), citing a 'national security strategy.' Given the severe stated reasons (terrorism, arms smuggling) and the broader geopolitical context of this expansion, it is highly unlikely that diplomatic efforts will succeed in completely reversing the policy and reopening the Kwara borders within the short remaining timeframe (30 days until July 1, 2025). Historically, West African border closures involving national security or major diplomatic rifts take many months to resolve. While the Nigerian House of Representatives has called for diplomatic intervention, there is no evidence of a breakthrough as of May 31, 2025.

There is overwhelming, high-quality evidence indicating that a coordinated diplomatic effort explicitly aimed at recognizing a Palestinian state will take place in mid-June 2025. French President Emmanuel Macron and Prime Minister François Bayrou have both publicly stated that France plans to announce formal recognition of a Palestinian state during an international conference co-chaired with Saudi Arabia, scheduled for June 17-20, 2025. Furthermore, reliable reports confirm that both the UK and Canada have joined this initiative and intend to make a joint announcement. Given that none of these three countries currently recognizes Palestine, the announcement of recognition by any one of them would trigger a YES resolution. The timeline is highly specific and explicitly targets June 2025. While there is a minor risk that diplomatic pressure, geopolitical events, or a semantic shift (e.g., announcing a "roadmap to recognition" instead of immediate statehood) could delay or dilute the event, the sheer momentum behind this G7-led initiative makes failure or long delay unlikely. Therefore, the probability is assessed at 88%.

There is overwhelming evidence indicating that one or more UN member countries will formally recognize Palestine in June 2025. A UN-backed 'two-state solution' conference led by France and Saudi Arabia is scheduled for mid-June (around June 17-20). In the lead-up, France has been aggressively lobbying the UK, Canada, Belgium, and the Netherlands to partake in a joint recognition of Palestinian statehood at this conference. More concretely, on May 25, 2025, Malta's Prime Minister Robert Abela explicitly confirmed that Malta will officially recognize the State of Palestine on June 20. With multiple countries 'determined' to coordinate this in June and Malta specifically committing to a firmly scheduled date, the likelihood that at least one nation follows through within the calendar month is extremely high. The only remaining risk is a coordinated, last-minute delay to July due to intense diplomatic pressure or a postponement of the UN conference, but the sheer number of countries involved makes a total failure to act very unlikely.

The border closures between Benin and Nigeria's Kwara State began on May 7, 2025, due to severe security concerns, including arms smuggling and the emergence of a new terror group ("Mahmuda"). Rather than showing signs of rapid resolution, Benin's response has escalated throughout May, with closures extending to Lagos (May 10) and Ogun (May 21). The physical nature of the blockade (involving heavy stones) and the multi-ministry coordination suggest this is a formalized, non-temporary policy. Historically, border closures in West Africa driven by security or trade disputes (such as Nigeria's own 2019 border closures) generally take many months or even years to resolve. With the resolution date of July 1, 2025, only one month away and no current evidence of breakthrough bilateral diplomacy, the chances of the border being credibly reported as reopened before the deadline are extremely low. I estimate a 5% probability out of an abundance of caution for unforeseen sudden diplomatic interventions.
